food_subgroup: Baked flatbread with meat and egg topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Turkish pide with sujuk and egg. FOODON_03302757 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Flat bread) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for pide with sujuk and egg toppings in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 (Flatbread) is the closest available parent concept. No specific concept for pide with sujuk and egg exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole pide with sujuk and egg, approximately 350 g, as typically served at a Turkish restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Pita bread (SR Legacy 18413), Beef sausage (SR Legacy 13961), Whole egg cooked (SR Legacy 01129), Cheddar cheese (SR Legacy 01009)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for pide with sujuk and egg exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for pita bread (~50% by weight), spiced beef sausage/sujuk (~25%), whole cooked egg (~15%), and cheddar cheese (~10%), reflecting a typical restaurant-style pide. Approximate per-100g values derived from weighted blend. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Pita bread, white — GI value 57 (Atkinson et al., 2008); analogous estimate applied to pide flatbread base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish pide with sujuk and egg. The GI of 57 is taken from white pita bread (Atkinson et al., 2008, AJCN 88:1498S–1503S), which is the closest structurally analogous leavened flatbread in the University of Sydney GI Database. The protein and fat content from sujuk and egg will further attenuate the glycemic response in practice, so 57 represents a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 34.3, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 24.5 × 3.5 = 85.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1820 mg per full serving) from sujuk seasoning, cheese, and bread
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load per serving, which can approach or exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal; portion reduction and requesting low-sodium preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (57) with high glycemic load per full serving (~34) due to the refined flatbread base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in small portions, as the refined pide dough contributes a medium GI and a high glycemic load per full serving; pairing with a salad and limiting portion to half a pide can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~4.6 g per 100 g) and sodium (~520 mg per 100 g) from sujuk and cheese, with trans fat present from processed sausage
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium from the sujuk and cheese toppings, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; occasional small-portion consumption is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pide with Sujuk and Egg (Turkish: Pide Sücük Yumurtalı) is a traditional Turkish baked flatbread dish originating from Turkey and widely popular across the Middle East and Turkish diaspora communities. The dish consists of an oval or boat-shaped leavened flatbread dough (pide) that is topped with sujuk — a heavily spiced, dried fermented beef sausage seasoned with garlic, cumin, black pepper, and red pepper — along with cracked whole eggs and melted cheese, then baked in a high-temperature oven until the crust is golden and crisp at the edges while remaining soft and chewy in the centre. The flavour profile is bold and savoury, with the smoky, garlicky heat of the sujuk complementing the richness of the egg yolk and the mild creaminess of the melted cheese. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense (~248 kcal per 100 g), providing meaningful protein (~12.5 g) and fat (~11.8 g) from the sujuk and egg, alongside carbohydrates (~24.5 g) from the flatbread base. The sodium content is notably high (~520 mg per 100 g), making a full serving a significant sodium load for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium), based on the white pita bread analogue, though the protein and fat from toppings moderate the glycemic response in practice. This dish is suitable for halal diets and contains gluten, egg, and dairy allergens. In Singapore, it is served at Middle Eastern and Turkish restaurants such as Sahara Bar & Restaurant.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for pide with sujuk and egg exists in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy to white pita bread from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: pita bread (FDC 18413, ~50% weight), beef sausage/sujuk analogue (FDC 13961, ~25% weight), cooked whole egg (FDC 01129, ~15% weight), and cheddar cheese (FDC 01009, ~10% weight). The input record described the topping as 'Beef Pepperoni slices' but sujuk is the canonical Turkish ingredient; beef pepperoni was used as a proxy for sujuk in the USDA SR Legacy composite given the absence of a sujuk-specific USDA entry. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the same USDA SR Legacy composite and are not individually confirmed from a single source entry; these values carry additional uncertainty. The food_group in the input was listed as 'Dairy and eggs' but has been corrected to 'Mixed' as this dish spans flatbread, meat, egg, and dairy components. The is_halal flag is set to true based on the dish description using beef sujuk and no pork ingredients; however, halal certification of the specific restaurant has not been independently verified. GI glycemic load per serving = (57 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 34.3, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 24.5 × 3.5 = 85.75 g.
